title: Our Insatiable Appetite for Stories About Cannibalism
description: Why do the characters in so many new novels insist upon eating each other?
hook: Cannibalism is everywhere in new fiction. What does our obsession with consumption—literal and metaphorical—say about us right now?

title: Our Plastic-Surgery Nightmare
description: As cosmetic procedures become both more invisible and more extreme, our connection to reality is fraying.
hook: Cosmetic procedures are becoming invisible while getting more extreme. We're losing the ability to read faces—and maybe each other.

title: Look Closer: Pieter De Hooch’s Puzzle Box
description: What the painter, a contemporary of Vermeer, revealed and concealed in a beguiling 1663 work
hook: A Vermeer contemporary painted layers of revelation and concealment into one beguiling canvas. Decoder needed.

title: Lit Hub Weekly: July 6 – 10, 2026
description: Lisa Owens explores the “taboo” ways women writers arrange the balance between creativity with family. | Lit Hub Craft How Jane Austen subverted the kind of romance she mastered by writing Emma. | Lit Hub Biography How Joyce Carol Oates,
hook: Women writers on the taboo math of balancing motherhood and creativity—no fairy tale solutions offered here.

title: ‘Ghost Town’, ‘At The Club’ & Britain’s Summer of Discontent
description:
A disintegrating Specials were part of the sound of July 1981 as unrest flared up in Handsworth, Toxteth, Southall, Moss Side, Hyson Green & Brixton, as Ghost Town hit #1 45 years ago today, writes Wrongtom

Brixton 1981 by Kim Aldis

On 10 July 1981 the producer Lloyd Coxsone was minding his own business on Atlantic Road in the heart
hook: The Specials' dissolution soundtrack Britain's summer of unrest in 1981. Politics, rage, and the end of something in every note.

title: ‘Industry’ Emmy Snub: Prestige TV Is a Moniker Series Are Born with but Rarely Earn — Opinion
description: What is "awards-worthy" has become a self-selective brand that leaves little room for young showrunners and for series that grow into greatness.
hook: Industry got snubbed. A sharp essay on why prestige TV's gatekeeping rewards pedigree over shows that actually improve.

title: Take Care: Georgia Bernstein on Night Nurse
description: I could tell by the title sequence alone—the camera gliding along a body, following the path of a telephone cord—that Night Nurse (2026) would not be like the other movies I had been seeing at Sundance. While the festival in aggregate leans toward safe picks, Night Nurse, which premiered in the NEXT competition, is anything but.
hook: One title sequence—a camera following a phone cord along skin—told her everything about Night Nurse's visual language.
